_note: Chapter 3 — Mona in Florida. The strip -> Eddy's pitch -> Prior -> leaving.

c3_squat.name: The Squat
c3_squat.desc: A room over a dead arcade: mattress, hotplate, Eddy's schemes taped to the wall in his careful loser's handwriting. The ocean comes through the floorboards as a smell. Home, insofar as the word applies.

interactions.label: Read Eddy's empire on the wall
pages: Eddy has drawn corporations as boxes and himself as arrows passing through them. Every scheme ends in a clean apartment, a legitimate number, clothes that close their own seams. None includes the cost of getting there. Your name appears only as M, beside estimated weekly income.

Under the newest diagram is an older one: a route from Cleveland, crossed out after Lanette disappeared. Eddy keeps failed futures because paper makes them look pending. You realize the wall is not a plan. It is camouflage over the fact that both of you have been standing still.

c3_strip.name: The Strip — Night
c3_strip.desc: Neon syrup over cracked stucco, security drones like patient wasps, music from three bars fighting over the same block. You know every doorway's angle and every cop's schedule. Working knowledge, in the exact sense.

interactions.label: Listen past the strip's commercial noise
pages: Between club loops, a pirate signal leaks from an open service hatch: a woman reciting freight weather, missing girls, and police frequencies over a guitar recorded before the coast died. The station calls itself Texas though the transmitter is probably three blocks away.

One missing-girl description fits you until the age, then fits Lanette until the city. Nobody on the strip stops walking. The signal repeats its address for replies, but SINless girls do not send replies. You memorize it anyway—the private reflex of someone beginning to suspect she may survive long enough to need witnesses.

c3_bar.name: Hooky Green's
c3_bar.desc: Cane furniture going gray, a ceiling fan stirring the heat around, and a suit at the corner table who doesn't sweat: Prior. English, maybe. Money, definitely. He looks at you the way buyers look at samples.

c3_beach.name: Dead Ocean Boardwalk
c3_beach.desc: Grey waves that leave a film, gulls that learned not to bother. You come here when Eddy's voice gets too loud in the squat. The horizon doesn't want anything from you, which makes it the best company in Florida.

c3_lobby.name: Hotel — Real Lobby
c3_lobby.desc: Air conditioning like a verdict, marble that's never had blood on it, staff who look through Eddy and see exactly what he is. Prior's card opens everything. Nobody has ever spent this kind of money on you without an invoice attached.
c3_lobby.locked_text: No suit, no suite. The meeting at Hooky's comes first.

interactions.label: Audit what Prior has purchased
pages: The room key carries a temporary identity with your photograph softened toward respectability. New clothes wait in three sizes. A clinic menu offers bone, skin, endocrine, and dental packages without prices. The minibar recognizes Eddy by name although he never gave it.

This is not generosity assembled in a hurry. Prior's people modeled you before Hooky Green's—your gait, measurements, habits, dependencies. Eddy sees proof that his pitch worked. You see a receiving department confirming that the shipment matches its advance notice.
